d9s0.what: The 10-acre headquarters of the Latter-day Saints and the reason this city exists. The granite Salt Lake Temple took 40 years to build (1853–1893) — ox-drawn granite blocks, 30 km from the quarry. Non-members can't enter temples, but the grounds, the Tabernacle and the visitor centers are open and genuinely welcoming.
see: The Tabernacle (1867): a wooden dome built without nails, acoustics so perfect a pin dropped at the pulpit is audible 50 m back — organists demonstrate at noon most days on the 11,623-pipe organ
The temple exterior — note the long-running seismic renovation; scaffolding may still wrap parts of it
The Beehive House and Eagle Gate a block east — Brigham Young's 1854 home
tips: An hour is right; guides (young missionaries from everywhere on Earth) offer free tours — take one or politely decline, both are fine
City Creek Center across the street: A/C, food court, and a creek running through a shopping mall
Then laundry, cooler restock, fuel — the boring hour that buys you a smooth desert week
d9s0.fact: The temple's foundation was buried and hidden for years during a federal standoff in 1858 — when dug up, the sandstone had cracked, so they restarted in granite. Hence 40 years.

d9s1.what: If you roll in before dark, this is your teaser: Bryce Canyon's rim at 2,400 m, looking down into the Silent City — thousands of stone spires glowing orange in the last light. Full immersion is tomorrow at dawn; tonight is just the trailer.
see: The Silent City from the rim rail — the densest hoodoo field on Earth
Thor's Hammer silhouetted at last light
tips: You're at 2,400 m now — expect to feel the stairs and bring the fleece; it can drop near 5°C at night even in July
If you miss the light, skip it — sunrise tomorrow is the main event and it's better
d9s1.fact: Bryce has some of the darkest night skies in the lower 48 — if you step out late tonight, the Milky Way is a structure, not a smudge.

d10s0.what: Bryce isn't a canyon — it's the eroding edge of a plateau, retreating amphitheater by amphitheater. Hoodoos form because this limestone freezes and thaws 200 nights a year: water wedges cracks open by day, ice pries them wider by night, and acid rain melts the glue between layers. Sunrise Point at dawn is the single best light show of your entire trip.
see: First light hitting the Silent City — the hoodoos ignite orange-pink from the top down, over about 20 minutes
The 'windows' in the fins — hoodoos being born; a fin becomes windows becomes spires
Look east: on clear mornings you can see 150+ km to Navajo Mountain
tips: Be on the rim 15 min before the posted sunrise (~6:10am) — the pre-light color is half the show
It is COLD at 2,400 m at dawn, even mid-July: full fleece, hat if you have one, coffee in a thermos
The rim path between Sunrise and Sunset Points is flat — wander it while the light works
d10s0.fact: Paiute tradition holds the hoodoos are the Legend People, turned to stone by Coyote for their misdeeds — the rows of red figures at dawn make it obvious why. The Paiute name means 'red rocks standing like men in a bowl-shaped canyon.'

d10s1.what: The best short hike in Utah, official: descend among the hoodoos via Queen's Garden, come back up through Navajo Loop's slot — 4.6 km total, 180 m down and up. Down in the maze the spires tower 20 storeys over you and the silence is total.
see: Queen Victoria hoodoo (the trail's namesake — she's there, in profile, riding side-saddle)
Wall Street: the Navajo Loop's finale — a slot canyon with 200-year-old Douglas firs growing in the crack, reaching for a ribbon of sky
Thor's Hammer up close from the Navajo switchbacks
tips: Direction matters: DOWN Queen's Garden (from Sunrise Point), UP Wall Street (to Sunset Point), then the flat rim path back — gentler descent, dramatic exit
Start by 7am: shade, cool air, empty trail; by 10am it's a hot conga line
2,400 m altitude — you'll puff on the climb out; that's the elevation, not your fitness
1 L of water each minimum; there's none below the rim
d10s1.fact: The whole plateau retreats about 1–4 m per century. The rim you stand on this morning will not exist in a few thousand years — Bryce is the most temporary-feeling permanent thing you'll ever see.

d10s2.what: The 1.7-km Zion–Mt Carmel tunnel (1930) was the engineering marvel of its day — blasted through a sheer sandstone cliff, with gallery windows cut sideways so drivers could dump rubble during construction and passengers could peek after. The switchbacks below it drop you into Zion Canyon like a landing airplane.
see: Checkerboard Mesa just before the tunnel — sandstone crosshatched by ancient dune layers and modern frost cracks
The gallery windows flashing canyon views as you drive the tunnel (passenger's job)
Bighorn sheep on the slickrock east of the tunnel — this stretch is the best odds in Utah
tips: Your pickup fits fine (oversize vehicles need a paid escort; you're not one)
Headlights on, sunglasses off — it's genuinely dark inside
Pullouts after the switchbacks are worth one stop for the Great Arch view back up
d10s2.fact: When it opened in 1930 it was the longest road tunnel in America, and the road it completed (the 'road to nowhere,' critics said) is why Zion went from 4,000 visitors a year to 5 million.

d10s3.what: Springdale is a one-street town wedged between 600-m sandstone walls — the entire town is inside the canyon's mouth. Tonight it has one job for you: Narrows gear.
see: The Watchman peak glowing red over town at sunset — shoot it from the Canyon Junction bridge if you're feeling it
Gallery-and-gear row along Zion Park Blvd
tips: Rent Narrows packages TONIGHT (canyoneering boots + neoprene socks + wooden staff, ~$30/person): Zion Outfitter, Zion Adventures and others are open till 8–9pm; pickup tonight = you make the first shuttle tomorrow
Ask the shop for tomorrow's flash-flood rating and water flow (CFS) while you're there — they post it daily
Buy tomorrow's lunch tonight; nothing is open at 5:30am
d10s3.fact: Springdale's permanent population is about 500; on a July day 15,000+ people move through it. The town shuttle exists because the traffic once backed up 5 km into the desert.

d11s0.what: Zion is a canyon you enter from the bottom, looking up — the inverse of the Grand Canyon — and in summer the scenic drive belongs to shuttles only. The Virgin River did all of this: a modest desert stream that drops so steeply it cuts like a bandsaw, 300+ m into Navajo sandstone.
see: From the shuttle window (left side going in): the Court of the Patriarchs, Angels Landing's spine, the Great White Throne
Ride to the LAST stop (Temple of Sinawava) first thing and work backwards — everyone else does it forwards
tips: Be in the shuttle line by 5:45 for the ~6am first bus; by 9am the wait can hit an hour
Park at the visitor center OR walk/town-shuttle from your Springdale hotel — hotel is usually easier
Zion is the one place this trip where crowds genuinely alter the experience — your dawn start is the whole strategy
d11s0.fact: Mormon settlers named it Zion — refuge. When Brigham Young visited and found tobacco and wine in use, he declared it 'not Zion,' and locals cheerfully called it Not Zion for years. The name without the Not stuck.

d11s1.what: The Narrows is the canyon at its narrowest — walls 300 m tall, river 6 m wide — and the trail IS the river: you wade upstream in the Virgin, sometimes shin-deep, sometimes to your thighs, in the most famous slot canyon walk on Earth. July water is ~15–18°C: shocking for one minute, perfect after ten.
see: Riverside Walk (1.7 km paved) delivers you to the river entrance
Mystery Falls (30 min in) — a ribbon waterfall down the wall
Wall Street begins ~1.5 h upstream: the walls close to a hallway and the sky becomes a crack — THE Narrows photo; go at least this far if legs allow
Big Springs (~4 h round trip point) is the legal turnaround without a permit
tips: The rented boots + staff are not optional-feeling once you're in: the riverbed is greased bowling balls
Phone in the waterproof pouch, snacks in a dry bag; keep camera ready — light beams hit the water mid-morning
Any storm ANYWHERE upstream = flash flood risk; if rangers post a warning, believe them (the river can rise a metre in minutes)
Back by 1pm: pool, ice cream, and the A/C nap you'll have earned
d11s1.fact: Those walls are petrified sand dunes — 2,000 m of Jurassic desert stacked and cemented, then sliced by a stream you can step across. Given 10 million more years, the river intends to erase them entirely.

d12s0.what: A 3.2-hectare artificial lake on the Strip where 1,214 fountains dance to music every half hour (every 15 min in the evening), throwing water 140 m with air-cannon 'shooters' you feel in your chest. Free, and unimprovably Vegas: the desert's scarcest resource, flung skyward for applause.
see: At least two shows — the playlist rotates (opera to Elvis); the view from the center of the sidewalk rail is best
The Bellagio Conservatory inside (also free): a botanical set-piece rebuilt seasonally with hundreds of thousands of flowers
Look up behind you: the Eiffel Tower replica frames the fountains from Paris Las Vegas
tips: Shows run to midnight; full darkness (after ~8:30pm in July) is when the lights matter
Street parking doesn't exist; Bellagio self-parking is free for the first hour-ish, or walk the Strip from your hotel
41°C outside — do the Strip after dinner, museum-hop casinos for the A/C between landmarks
d12s0.fact: The lake uses water from wells the resort owns — recycled and, per the engineers, less than the golf course that occupied this land before 1998.

d12s1.what: The Sphere (2023): 111 m tall, wrapped in 54,000 m² of programmable LED — the largest screen on Earth. From outside it becomes an eyeball, a planet, a pumpkin, a fishbowl; the exterior art rotates all night and the whole thing cost more than most airports ($2.3 billion).
see: The exosphere animations — best sightlines from the Venetian's pedestrian bridges or the top of the Palazzo garage (free, unofficial, popular)
The 'blink' — when the eyeball looks at you, the crowd audibly reacts
tips: Exterior is free and visible from half the Strip; going inside (concerts/'Sphere Experience') is a separate wallet decision
It's a 15-min walk from the Strip proper — pair it with the Venetian's canals for A/C on the way
d12s1.fact: There are 1.2 million LED pucks out there, each with 48 diodes, spaced 20 cm apart — the resolution is calibrated for viewing from the freeway.

d12s2.what: Old Vegas: Fremont Street is where the town began in 1905 and where the neon went when the Strip got tasteful. The Viva Vision canopy — 420 m long, 12 m up — runs hourly light shows over five blocks of vintage casinos, street performers, and zero pretension.
see: The canopy show on the hour after dark
Binion's and the Golden Nugget — 1950s Vegas still operating (the Nugget lobby displays a 27 kg gold nugget, real)
The restored neon: Vegas Vic the waving cowboy has been up since 1951
SlotZilla zipliners flying overhead the length of the street
tips: 20 min drive from the Strip; rideshare beats parking here
It's grittier and looser than the Strip — that's the appeal; keep wallets in front pockets
Cheap, huge, deep-fried everything if the buffet didn't finish you
d12s2.fact: Fremont was the first paved street in Las Vegas and its casinos ran the first licenses in Nevada — the Strip is technically not even in the City of Las Vegas; this is.

d13s0.what: A 1935 Art Deco temple to public science on the shoulder of Mount Hollywood — free since opening because Griffith J. Griffith's will demanded it. The terrace view at sunset is the definitive look at Los Angeles: 1,200 km² of city igniting light by light, the Hollywood Sign to your right, the Pacific burning behind Santa Monica.
see: Sunset from the south terraces, then STAY 30 min — the city-lights phase beats the sunset itself
The Foucault pendulum knocking over pins as the Earth rotates under it, the Tesla coil, and the basement's scale-model solar system
Public telescopes on the lawn and roof after dark — volunteers aim them at whatever's up; free, queue moves fast
The Hollywood Sign (1923): erected as 'HOLLYWOODLAND,' an illuminated real-estate ad meant to last 18 months
tips: Parking is the boss fight: the summit lot is paid and fills before sunset — arrive by 6:30pm, or park down Vermont Ave and walk up 15 min
Bring the fleece one more time: LA evenings run cool up here
The café is fine; better plan is In-N-Out after (10 min down the hill)
d13s0.fact: Griffith donated the land and observatory money after (and partly to atone for) shooting his wife — she survived, he did two years in San Quentin, and LA got its best public building out of the scandal. The city debated refusing the gift.

d13s1.what: The original In-N-Out opened in Baldwin Park in 1948 — California's first drive-thru burger stand, still family-owned, menu unchanged and secret menu memorized by the entire state. This is the first-timer's rite of passage, conveniently 10 minutes from your hotel.
see: Order: Double-Double Animal Style (mustard-grilled patties, extra spread, grilled onions), fries 'well-done,' a Neapolitan shake if you're showing off
The palm trees out front are planted in an X — founder Harry Snyder loved 'It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World,' where treasure is buried under a big W of palms
tips: 'Animal Style' isn't on the menu board; just say it, they'll nod
Drive-thru line looks apocalyptic but moves absurdly fast — that's the company's whole engineering obsession
d13s1.fact: Everything is made in view: no freezers, no microwaves, no heat lamps exist in any store — potatoes are cut in the shop and the beef never freezes. It's why there's no In-N-Out east of Texas: every store must sit within a day of a patty plant.

d14s0.what: A working film studio that happens to run a theme park on top — the Studio Tour has trundled past active soundstages since 1964, and the rides are the movies weaponized. With one day: ruthless priorities, mobile ordering, and shade strategy.
see: Super Nintendo World FIRST at rope drop: Mario Kart: Bowser's Challenge (AR headset kart racing) before the wait hits 90 min; grab a virtual queue for the land itself in the app if required that day
Studio Tour (60 min, midday when it's hottest — you're seated and shaded): King Kong 360 3-D, the Fast & Furious finale, the Psycho house, Jaws, the real War of the Worlds plane-crash set
Lower Lot: Jurassic World (you WILL get wet — perfect at 2pm), Transformers, The Mummy (best pure coaster in the park)
Hogwarts in the afternoon: Forbidden Journey is the best dark ride ever built; frozen Butterbeer, non-negotiable
WaterWorld stunt show — the sleeper hit; sit in the splash zone or regret your dryness
tips: Be at the gates 8:30–8:45 for a 9am open; the first two hours equal the next six
Single-rider lines cut waits by half or more on Mario Kart, Transformers, Mummy, Jurassic
Download the app now: live wait times + mobile food ordering; refillable Coke cup pays for itself by 1pm
The park is built on a hill in two tiers connected by the 'Starway' escalators — plan Lower Lot as ONE visit, not three
d14s0.fact: Nintendo of Japan supervised Super Nintendo World down to the timing of the coin-block 'ding' — the Power-Up Band mini-games hide a real boss fight (Bowser Jr.) that most visitors never find. Ask a team member in the land.

d15s0.what: The 1909 Santa Monica Pier is the oldest pleasure pier on the West Coast and the ceremonial end of Route 66 — 'End of the Trail,' says the sign, 3,940 km from Grant Park in Chicago, where you started driving 15 days ago. You'll drive the mother road's surviving stretches home; today you touch the terminus.
see: The Route 66 'End of the Trail' sign (mid-pier) — the photo that bookends your trip
The 1922 Looff Hippodrome carousel building (National Historic Landmark) and its hand-carved horses
Pacific Park's Ferris wheel — the world's only solar-powered one; $10-ish and worth it at sunset
Fishermen on the far end pulling up mackerel while pelicans supervise
tips: Beach parking lots south of the pier are easiest (~$15); arrive from 10am, swim mid-day, pier at sunset
The Pacific in July is ~19°C — brisk for 90 seconds, then glorious
Tacos: the stands on the boardwalk toward Venice beat the pier restaurants
d15s0.fact: Route 66 never officially ended here — the real terminus was an intersection downtown — but a 2009 ceremony moved the symbolic sign to the sea because everyone agreed a road called 'America's Main Street' should end at the ocean.

d15s1.what: Abbot Kinney built actual canals here in 1905 to conjure 'Venice of America,' complete with gondoliers. Most were paved for cars, but the surviving six blocks are LA's secret garden — and the boardwalk out front is the city's id: bodybuilders, buskers, chainsaw jugglers, and the skatepark where modern skateboarding was invented.
see: Venice Skatepark — sunken concrete bowls on the sand; the locals flying out of them are ex-pros and future ones
Muscle Beach outdoor gym (Arnold's alma mater)
The canals: walk 3 blocks inland at 25th/Dell Ave — ducks, footbridges, absurd real estate, total silence
Boardwalk murals, including the Jim Morrison one (The Doors formed here)
tips: Walk or beach-path bike from Santa Monica (30 min on foot along the sand) rather than re-parking
The boardwalk is theatre — enjoy performers, tip if you photograph them, keep phones zipped
Sunset drink pick: rooftop at Hotel Erwin overlooks the whole circus
d15s1.fact: Kinney's Venice had gondoliers imported from Italy and a miniature railroad; when oil was struck in 1929, derricks stood between the canals for decades. LA has been at least four different cities on this exact sand.

d16s0.what: The original — Walt Disney's own park, opened 1955, the only Disney park he ever walked. It packs more landmark rides per hectare than anywhere on Earth into a hub-and-spoke of eight lands around Sleeping Beauty Castle. This is the bonus day that pushed the whole itinerary back 24 hours; make it count.
see: Star Wars: Rise of the Resistance at rope drop — a 20-minute, multi-stage attraction that is less 'ride' than 'being inside the film'; Galaxy's Edge itself is the best-built land in California
Space Mountain (Tomorrowland) second — the 1977 indoor coaster in the dark that every later space coaster copies
Indiana Jones Adventure — the enhanced-motion jeep ride Universal's been chasing for 30 years
The classics spine: Pirates of the Caribbean → Haunted Mansion → it's a small world (Walt-era originals, all)
Fireworks over the castle ~9:30pm (weather permitting) — the full-circle moment after Universal
tips: Mickey & Friends garage by 7:15 for the 8am open — security + tram to the esplanade eats 40 min; I-5 south from Burbank is 50–75 min in the morning
Lightning Lane Multi Pass (~$30+/person) pays for itself by noon on a July day; book return windows via the Disneyland app while you walk
Mobile-order lunch 45 min ahead; Blue Bayou (inside Pirates) needs a reservation — walk-up bar is the hack
Midday heat plan: indoor theater shows + Tiki Room (Dole Whip!) 1–3pm, then back out
Tomorrow you drive 440 miles — fireworks, then straight home and pack the truck
d16s0.fact: Walt kept a private apartment above the Main Street firehouse and a lamp burns in its window to this day — lit every night in his honor. The park's 1955 opening day was such a disaster (counterfeit tickets, melting asphalt, a plumbers' strike that forced Walt to choose: working toilets OR drinking fountains) that the company later celebrated it as 'Black Sunday.'
